The post-condition\\n1177:      * of <code>flush()</code> is that any previously sent record will have completed (e.g. <code>Future.isDone() == true</code>\\n1178:      * and callbacks passed to {@link #send(ProducerRecord,Callback)} have been called).\\n1179:      * A request is considered completed when it is successfully acknowledged\\n1180:      * according to the <code>acks</code> configuration you have specified or else it results in an error.\\n1181:      * <p>\\n1182:      * Other threads can continue sending records while one thread is blocked waiting for a flush call to complete,\\n1183:      * however no guarantee is made about the completion of records sent after the flush call begins.\\n1184:      * <p>\\n1185:      * This method can be useful when consuming from some input system and producing into Kafka. The <code>flush()</code> call\\n1186:      * gives a convenient way to ensure all previously sent messages have actually completed.\\n1187:      * <p>\\n1188:      * This example shows how to consume from one Kafka topic and produce to another Kafka topic:\\n1189:      * <pre>\\n1190:      * {@code\\n1191:      * for(ConsumerRecord<String, String> record: consumer.poll(100))\\n1192:      *     producer.send(new ProducerRecord(\\\"my-topic\\\", record.key(), record.value());\\n1193:      * producer.flush();\\n1194:      * consumer.commitSync();\\n1195:      * }\\n1196:      * </pre>\\n1197:      *\\n1198:      * Note that the above example may drop records if the produce request fails. The post-condition\n1177:      * of <code>flush()</code> is that any previously sent record will have completed (e.g. <code>Future.isDone() == true</code>\n1178:      * and callbacks passed to {@link #send(ProducerRecord,Callback)} have been called).\n1179:      * A request is considered completed when it is successfully acknowledged\n1180:      * according to the <code>acks</code> configuration you have specified or else it results in an error.\n1181:      * <p>\n1182:      * Other threads can continue sending records while one thread is blocked waiting for a flush call to complete,\n1183:      * however no guarantee is made about the completion of records sent after the flush call begins.\n1184:      * <p>\n1185:      * This method can be useful when consuming from some input system and producing into Kafka. The <code>flush()</code> call\n1186:      * gives a convenient way to ensure all previously sent messages have actually completed.\n1187:      * <p>\n1188:      * This example shows how to consume from one Kafka topic and produce to another Kafka topic:\n1189:      * <pre>\n1190:      * {@code\n1191:      * for(ConsumerRecord<String, String> record: consumer.poll(100))\n1192:      *     producer.send(new ProducerRecord(\"my-topic\", record.key(), record.value());\n1193:      * producer.flush();\n1194:      * consumer.commitSync();\n1195:      * }\n1196:      * </pre>\n1197:      *\n1198:      * Note that the above example may drop records if the produce request fails. If we want to ensure that this does not occur\n1199:      * we need to set <code>retries=&lt;large_number&gt;</code> in our config.\n1200:      * </p>\n1201:      * <p>\n1202:      * Applications don't need to call this method for transactional producers, since the {@link #commitTransaction()} will\n1203:      * flush all buffered records before performing the commit. This ensures that all the {@link #send(ProducerRecord)}\n1204:      * calls made since the previous {@link #beginTransaction()} are completed before the commit.\n1205:      * </p>\n1206:      * <p>\n1207:      * <b>Important:</b> This method must not be called from within the callback provided to\n1208:      * {@link #send(ProducerRecord, Callback)}. Invoking <code>flush()</code> in this context will result in a\n1209:      * {@link KafkaException} being thrown, as it will cause a deadlock.\n1210:      * </p>\n1211:      *\n1212:      * @throws InterruptException If the thread is interrupted while blocked\n1213:      * @throws KafkaException If the method is invoked inside a {@link #send(ProducerRecord, Callback)} callback\n1214:      */\n1215:     @Override\n1216:     public void flush() {\n1217:         if (Thread.currentThread() == this.ioThread) {\n1218:             log.error(\"KafkaProducer.flush() invocation inside a callback is not permitted because it may lead to deadlock.\");\n1219:             throw new KafkaException(\"KafkaProducer.flush() invocation inside a callback is not permitted because it may lead to deadlock.\");\n1220:         }\n1221: \n1222:         log.trace(\"Flushing accumulated records in producer.\");\n1223: \n1224:         long start = time.nanoseconds();\n1225:         this.accumulator.beginFlush();\n1226:         this.sender.wakeup();\n1227:         try {\n1228:             this.accumulator.awaitFlushCompletion();\n1229:         } catch (InterruptedException e) {\n1230:             throw new InterruptException(\"Flush interrupted.\", e);\n1231:         } finally {\n1232:             producerMetrics.recordFlush(time.nanoseconds() - start);\n1233:         }\n1234:     }\n1235: \n1236:     /**\n1237:      * Get the partition metadata for the given topic. If some partitions were excluded, we'd still want to\\n255:         // go through adaptive logic, even if we have one partition.\\n256:         // See also RecordAccumulator#partitionReady where the queueSizes are built.\\n257:         if (length < 1 || queueSizes.length < 2) {\\n258:             log.trace(\\\"The number of partitions is too small: available={}, all={}, not using adaptive for topic {}\\\",\\n259:                     length, queueSizes.length, topic);\\n260:             partitionLoadStats = null;\\n261:             return;\\n262:         }\\n263: \\n264:         // We build cumulative frequency table from the queue sizes in place. Then a uniformly distributed random variable\\n267:         // in the range [0..last) would map to a partition with weighted probability.\\n268:         // Example: suppose we have 3 partitions with the corresponding queue sizes:\\n269:         //  0 3 1\\n270:         // Then we can invert them by subtracting the queue size from the max queue size + 1 = 4:\\n271: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489@31d9200189f81283bf60e929ddc38fd68ac034e2/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/BuiltInPartitioner.java%3FL250-270\"}","structuredContent":{"content":"250:         // The queueSizes.length represents the number of all partitions in the topic and if we have\n251:         // less than 2 partitions, there is no need to do adaptive logic.\n252:         // If partitioner.availability.timeout.ms != 0, then partitions that experience high latencies\n253:         // (greater than partitioner.availability.timeout.ms) may be excluded, the length represents\n254:         // partitions that are not excluded. org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.internals.BatchAccumulator;\nimport org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.internals.BufferPool;\nimport org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.internals.ProducerBatch;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.Metrics;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.protocol.Errors;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.record.DefaultRecordBatch;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.record.Record;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.requests.MetadataResponse;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.utils.LogContext;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.utils.MockTime;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Time;\n\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Benchmark;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.BenchmarkMode;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Fork;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Level;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Measurement;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Mode;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.OutputTimeUnit;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Param;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Scope;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Setup;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.State;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.TearDown;\nimport org.openjdk.jmh.annotations.Warmup;\n\nimport java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;\nimport java.util.ArrayList;\nimport java.util.Collections;\nimport java.util.List;\nimport java.util.Map;\nimport java.util.Optional;\nimport java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;\nimport java.util.function.Function;\nimport java.util.stream.Collectors;\nimport java.util.stream.Stream;\n\n@State(Scope.Benchmark)\n@Fork(value = 1)\n@Warmup(iterations = 5)\n@Measurement(iterations = 15)\n@BenchmarkMode(Mode.AverageTime)\n@OutputTimeUnit(TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)\npublic class BatchAccumulatorFlushBenchmark {\n\n    private static final String TOPIC = \"test\";\n    private static final int PARTITION = 0;\n    private static final int BATCH_SIZE = 1024;\n    private static final long TOTAL_SIZE = 10 * 1024 * 1024;\n\n    @Param({\"5000\", \"10000\"})\n    private int numRecords;\n\n    private BatchAccumulator accum;\n    private Metrics metrics;\n    private TopicPartition tp;\n    private Time time;\n\n    @Setup(Level.Invocation)\n    public void setup() throws InterruptedException {\n        tp = new TopicPartition(TOPIC, PARTITION);\n        time = new MockTime();\n        metrics = new Metrics(time);\n\n        Cluster cluster = createTestCluster();\n        accum = createBatchAccumulator();\n\n        appendRecords(cluster);\n        prepareFlush();\n    }\n\n    @TearDown(Level.Invocation)\n    public void tearDown() {\n        deallocateBatches();\n        if (metrics != null) {\n            metrics.close();\n        }\n    }\n\n    @Benchmark\n    public void measureFlushCompletion() throws InterruptedException {\n        accum.awaitFlushCompletion();\n    }\n\n    private Cluster createTestCluster() {\n        Node node = new Node(0, \"localhost\", 1111);\n        MetadataResponse.PartitionMetadata partMetadata = new MetadataResponse.PartitionMetadata(\n            Errors.NONE,\n            tp,\n            Optional.of(node.id()),\n            Optional.empty(),\n            null,\n            null,\n            null\n        );\n\n        Map<Integer, Node> nodes = Stream.of(node).collect(Collectors.toMap(Node::id, Function.identity()));\n        MetadataSnapshot metadataCache = new MetadataSnapshot(\n            null,\n            nodes,\n            Collections.singletonList(partMetadata),\n            Collections.emptySet(),\n            Collections.emptySet(),\n            Collections.emptySet(),\n            null,\n            Collections.emptyMap()\n        );\n        return metadataCache.cluster();\n    }\n\n    private BatchAccumulator createBatchAccumulator() {\n        return new BatchAccumulator(\n            new LogContext(),\n            BATCH_SIZE + DefaultRecordBatch.RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD,\n            Compression.NONE,\n            Integer.MAX_VALUE,  // lingerMs\n            100L,               // retryBackoffMs\n            1000L,              // retryBackoffMaxMs\n            3200,               // deliveryTimeoutMs\n            metrics,\n            \"producer-metrics\",\n            time,\n            null,\n            new BufferPool(TOTAL_SIZE, BATCH_SIZE, metrics, time, \"producer-metrics\")\n        );\n    }\n\n    private void appendRecords(Cluster cluster) throws InterruptedException {\n        byte[] key = \"key\".getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);\n        byte[] value = \"value\".getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);\n\n     Record.EMPTY_HEADERS,\n                null,\n                1000L,\n                time.milliseconds(),\n                cluster\n            );\n        }\n    }\n\n    private void java.net.InetSocketAddress;\\n86: import java.time.Duration;\\n87: import java.util.Arrays;\\n88: import java.util.Collections;\\n89: import java.util.List;\\n90: import java.util.Map;\\n91: import java.util.Objects;\\n92: import java.util.Optional;\\n93: import java.util.Properties;\\n94: import java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException;\\n95: import java.util.concurrent.Future;\\n96: import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;\\n97: import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicReference;\\n98: \\n99: \\n100: /**\\n101:  * A Kafka client that publishes records to the Kafka cluster.\\n102:  * <P>\\n103:  * The producer is <i>thread safe</i> and sharing a single producer instance across threads will generally be faster than\\n104:  * having multiple instances.\\n105:  * <p>\\n106:  * Here is a simple example of using the producer to send records with strings containing sequential numbers as the key/value\\n107:  * pairs.\\n108:  * <pre>\\n109:  * {@code\\n110:  * Properties props = new Properties();\\n111:  * props.put(\\\"bootstrap.servers\\\", \\\"localhost:9092\\\");\\n112:  * props.put(\\\"linger.ms\\\", 1);\\n113:  * props.put(\\\"key.serializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer\\\");\\n114:  * props.put(\\\"value.serializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer\\\");\\n115:  *\\n116:  * Producer<String, String> producer = new KafkaProducer<>(props);\\n117:  * for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)\\n118:  *     producer.send(new ProducerRecord<String, String>(\\\"my-topic\\\", Integer.toString(i), Integer.toString(i)));\\n119:  *\\n120:  * producer.close();\\n121:  * }</pre>\\n122:  * <p>\\n123:  * The producer consists of a pool of buffer space that holds records that haven't yet been transmitted to the server\\n124:  * as well as a background I/O thread that is responsible for turning these records into requests and transmitting them\\n125:  * to the cluster. Failure to close the producer after use will leak these resources.\\n126:  * <p>\\n127:  * The {@link #send(ProducerRecord) send()} method is asynchronous. When called, it adds the record to a buffer of pending record sends\\n128:  * and immediately returns. This allows the producer to batch together individual records for efficiency.\\n129:  * <p>\\n130:  * The <code>acks</code> config controls the criteria under which requests are considered complete. The default setting \\\"all\\\"\\n131:  * will result in blocking on the full commit of the record, the slowest but most durable setting.\\n132:  * <p>\\n133:  * If the request fails, the producer can automatically retry. The <code>retries</code> setting defaults to <code>Integer.MAX_VALUE</code>, and\\n134:  * it's recommended to use <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> to control retry behavior, instead of <code>retries</code>.\\n135:  * <p>\\n136:  * The producer maintains buffers of unsent records for each partition. These buffers are of a size specified by\\n137:  * the <code>batch.size</code> config. Making this larger can result in more batching, but requires more memory (since we will\\n138:  * generally have one of these buffers for each active partition).\\n139:  * <p>\\n140:  * By default a buffer is available to send immediately even if there is additional unused space in the buffer. However if you\\n141:  * want to reduce the number of requests you can set <code>linger.ms</code> to something greater than 0. This will\\n142:  * instruct the producer to wait up to that number of milliseconds before sending a request in hope that more records will\\n143:  * arrive to fill up the same batch. This is analogous to Nagle's algorithm in TCP. For example, in the code snippet above,\\n144:  * likely all 100 records would be sent in a single request since we set our linger time to 1 millisecond. However this setting\\n145:  * would add 1 millisecond of latency to our request waiting for more records to arrive if we didn't fill up the buffer. Note that\\n146:  * records that arrive close together in time will generally batch together even with <code>linger.ms=0</code>. So, under heavy load,\\n147:  * batching will occur regardless of the linger configuration; however setting this to something larger than 0 can lead to fewer, more\\n148:  * efficient requests when not under maximal load at the cost of a small amount of latency.\\n149:  * <p>\\n150:  * The <code>buffer.memory</code> controls the total amount of memory available to the producer for buffering. If records\\n151:  * are sent faster than they can be transmitted to the server then this buffer space will be exhausted. When the buffer space is\\n152:  * exhausted additional send calls will block. The threshold for time to block is determined by <code>max.block.ms</code> after which it returns\\n153:  * a failed future with BufferExhaustedException.\\n154:  * <p>\\n155:  * The <code>key.serializer</code> and <code>value.serializer</code> instruct how to turn the key and value objects the user provides with\\n156:  * their <code>ProducerRecord</code> into bytes. You can use the included {@link org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.ByteArraySerializer} or\\n157:  * {@link org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer} for simple byte or string types.\\n158:  * <p>\\n159:  * From Kafka 0.11, the KafkaProducer supports two additional modes: the idempotent producer and the transactional producer.\\n160:  * The idempotent producer strengthens Kafka's delivery semantics from at least once to exactly once delivery. In particular\\n161:  * producer retries will no longer introduce duplicates. The transactional producer allows an application to send messages\\n162:  * to multiple partitions (and topics!) atomically.\\n163:  * </p>\\n164:  * <p>\\n165:  * From Kafka 3.0, the <code>enable.idempotence</code> configuration defaults to true. When enabling idempotence,\\n166:  * <code>retries</code> config will default to <code>Integer.MAX_VALUE</code> and the <code>acks</code> config will\\n167:  * default to <code>all</code>. There are no API changes for the idempotent producer, so existing applications will\\n168:  * not need to be modified to take advantage of this feature.\\n169:  * </p>\\n170:  * <p>\\n171:  * To take advantage of the idempotent producer, it is imperative to avoid application level re-sends since these cannot\\n172:  * be de-duplicated. As such, if an application enables idempotence, it is recommended to leave the <code>retries</code>\\n173:  * config unset, as it will be defaulted to <code>Integer.MAX_VALUE</code>. Additionally, if a {@link #send(ProducerRecord)}\\n174:  * returns an error even with infinite retries (for instance if the message expires in the buffer before being sent),\\n175:  * then it is recommended to shut down the producer and check the contents of the last produced message to ensure that\\n176:  * it is not duplicated. Finally, the producer can only guarantee idempotence for messages sent within a single session.\\n177:  * </p>\\n178:  * <p>To use the transactional producer and the attendant APIs, you must set the <code>transactional.id</code>\\n179:  * configuration property. If the <code>transactional.id</code> is set, idempotence is automatically enabled along with\\n180:  * the producer configs which idempotence depends on. Further, topics which are included in transactions should be configured\\n181:  * for durability. In particular, the <code>replication.factor</code> should be at least <code>3</code>, and the\\n182:  * <code>min.insync.replicas</code> for these topics should be set to 2. Finally, in order for transactional guarantees\\n183:  * to be realized from end-to-end, the consumers must be configured to read only committed messages as well.\\n184:  * </p>\\n185:  * <p>\\n186:  * The purpose of the <code>transactional.id</code> is to enable transaction recovery across multiple sessions of a\\n187:  * single producer instance. It would typically be derived from the shard identifier in a partitioned, stateful, application.\\n188:  * As such, it should be unique to each producer instance running within a partitioned application.\\n189:  * </p>\\n190:  * <p>All the new transactional APIs are blocking and will throw exceptions on failure. The example\\n191:  * below illustrates how the new APIs are meant to be used. It is similar to the example above, except that all\\n192:  * 100 messages are part of a single transaction.\\n193:  * </p>\\n194:  * <p>\\n195:  * <pre>\\n196:  * {@code\\n197:  * Properties props = new Properties();\\n198:  * props.put(\\\"bootstrap.servers\\\", \\\"localhost:9092\\\");\\n199:  * props.put(\\\"transactional.id\\\", \\\"my-transactional-id\\\");\\n200:  * Producer<String, String> producer = new KafkaProducer<>(props, new StringSerializer(), new StringSerializer());\\n201:  *\\n202:  * producer.initTransactions();\\n203:  *\\n204:  * try {\\n205:  *     producer.beginTransaction();\\n206:  *     for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)\\n207:  *         producer.send(new ProducerRecord<>(\\\"my-topic\\\", Integer.toString(i), Integer.toString(i)));\\n208:  *     producer.commitTransaction();\\n209:  * } catch (ProducerFencedException | OutOfOrderSequenceException | AuthorizationException e) {\\n210:  *     // We can't recover from these exceptions, so our only option is to close the producer and exit.\\n211:  *     producer.close();\\n212:  * } catch (KafkaException e) {\\n213:  *     // For all other exceptions, just abort the transaction and try again.\\n214:  *     producer.abortTransaction();\\n215:  * }\\n216:  * producer.close();\\n217:  * } </pre>\\n218:  * </p>\\n219:  * <p>\\n220:  * As is hinted at in the example, there can be only one open transaction per producer. All messages sent between the\\n221:  * {@link #beginTransaction()} and {@link #commitTransaction()} calls will be part of a single transaction. When the\\n222:  * <code>transactional.id</code> is specified, all messages sent by the producer must be part of a transaction.\\n223:  * </p>\\n224:  * <p>\\n225:  * The transactional producer uses exceptions to communicate error states. In particular, it is not required\\n226:  * to specify callbacks for <code>producer.send()</code> or to call <code>.get()</code> on the returned Future: a\\n227:  * <code>KafkaException</code> would be thrown if any of the\\n228:  * <code>producer.send()</code> or transactional calls hit an irrecoverable error during a transaction. See the {@link #send(ProducerRecord)}\\n229:  * documentation for more details about detecting errors from a transactional send.\\n230:  * </p>\\n231:  * </p>By calling\\n232:  * <code>producer.abortTransaction()</code> upon receiving a <code>KafkaException</code> we can ensure that any\\n233:  * successful writes are marked as aborted, hence keeping the transactional guarantees.\\n234:  * </p>\\n235:  * <p>\\n236:  * This client can communicate with brokers that are version 0.10.0 or newer. Older or newer brokers may not support\\n237:  * certain client features.  For instance, the transactional APIs need broker versions 0.11.0 or later. You will receive an\\n238:  * <code>UnsupportedVersionException</code> when invoking an API that is not available in the running broker version.\\n239:  * </p>\\n240:  */\\n241: public class KafkaProducer<K, V> implements Producer<K, V> {\\n242: \\n243:     private final Logger log;\\n244:     private static final String JMX_PREFIX = \\\"kafka.producer\\\";\\n245:     public static final String NETWORK_THREAD_PREFIX = \\\"kafka-producer-network-thread\\\";\\n246:     public static final String PRODUCER_METRIC_GROUP_NAME = \\\"producer-metrics\\\";\\n247: \\n248:     private final String clientId;\\n249:     // Visible for testing\\n250:     final Metrics metrics;\\n251: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489@31d9200189f81283bf60e929ddc38fd68ac034e2/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/KafkaProducer.java%3FL50-250\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"4fe9fbe4-71ce-4dd3-9613-12ffb0835aef","uuid":"e9b2acde-4203-4564-9105-75ff2e910475","tool_use_result":{"content":"{\"content\":\"50: org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RetriableException;\\n56: import org.apache.kafka.common.errors.SerializationException;\\n57: import org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException;\\n58: import org.apache.kafka.common.internals.ClusterResourceListeners;\\n62: import org.apache.kafka.common.internals.Plugin;\\n63: import org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.KafkaMetric;\\n64: import org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.KafkaMetricsContext;\\n65: import org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.MetricConfig;\\n66: import org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.Metrics;\\n67: import org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.MetricsContext;\\n68: import org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.MetricsReporter;\\n69: import org.apache.kafka.common.metrics.Sensor;\\n70: import org.apache.kafka.common.record.AbstractRecords;\\n71: import org.apache.kafka.common.record.CompressionType;\\n72: import org.apache.kafka.common.record.RecordBatch;\\n73: import org.apache.kafka.common.requests.JoinGroupRequest;\\n74: import org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.Serializer;\\n75: import org.apache.kafka.common.telemetry.internals.ClientTelemetryReporter;\\n76: import org.apache.kafka.common.telemetry.internals.ClientTelemetryUtils;\\n77: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.AppInfoParser;\\n78: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.LogContext;\\n79: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Time;\\n80: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Timer;\\n81: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Utils;\\n82: \\n83: import org.slf4j.Logger;\\n84: \\n85: import java.net.InetSocketAddress;\\n86: import java.time.Duration;\\n87: import java.util.Arrays;\\n88: import java.util.Collections;\\n89: import java.util.List;\\n90: import java.util.Map;\\n91: import java.util.Objects;\\n92: import java.util.Optional;\\n93: import java.util.Properties;\\n94: import java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException;\\n95: import java.util.concurrent.Future;\\n96: import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;\\n97: import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicReference;\\n98: \\n99: \\n100: /**\\n101:  * A Kafka client that publishes records to the Kafka cluster.\\n102:  * <P>\\n103:  * The producer is <i>thread safe</i> and sharing a single producer instance across threads will generally be faster than\\n104:  * having multiple instances.\\n105:  * <p>\\n106:  * Here is a simple example of using the producer to send records with strings containing sequential numbers as the key/value\\n107:  * pairs.\\n108:  * <pre>\\n109:  * {@code\\n110:  * Properties props = new Properties();\\n111:  * props.put(\\\"bootstrap.servers\\\", \\\"localhost:9092\\\");\\n112:  * props.put(\\\"linger.ms\\\", 1);\\n113:  * props.put(\\\"key.serializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer\\\");\\n114:  * props.put(\\\"value.serializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer\\\");\\n115:  *\\n116:  * Producer<String, String> producer = new KafkaProducer<>(props);\\n117:  * for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)\\n118:  *     producer.send(new ProducerRecord<String, String>(\\\"my-topic\\\", Integer.toString(i), Integer.toString(i)));\\n119:  *\\n120:  * producer.close();\\n121:  * }</pre>\\n122:  * <p>\\n123:  * The producer consists of a pool of buffer space that holds records that haven't yet been transmitted to the server\\n124:  * as well as a background I/O thread that is responsible for turning these records into requests and transmitting them\\n125:  * to the cluster. Failure to close the producer after use will leak these resources.\\n126:  * <p>\\n127:  * The {@link #send(ProducerRecord) send()} method is asynchronous. When called, it adds the record to a buffer of pending record sends\\n128:  * and immediately returns. This allows the producer to batch together individual records for efficiency.\\n129:  * <p>\\n130:  * The <code>acks</code> config controls the criteria under which requests are considered complete. The default setting \\\"all\\\"\\n131:  * will result in blocking on the full commit of the record, the slowest but most durable setting.\\n132:  * <p>\\n133:  * If the request fails, the producer can automatically retry. The <code>retries</code> setting defaults to <code>Integer.MAX_VALUE</code>, and\\n134:  * it's recommended to use <code>delivery.timeout.ms</code> to control retry behavior, instead of <code>retries</code>.\\n135:  * <p>\\n136:  * The producer maintains buffers of unsent records for each partition. These buffers are of a size specified by\\n137:  * the <code>batch.size</code> config. Making this larger can result in more batching, but requires more memory (since we will\\n138:  * generally have one of these buffers for each active partition).\\n139:  * <p>\\n140:  * By default a buffer is available to send immediately even if there is additional unused space in the buffer. However if you\\n141:  * want to reduce the number of requests you can set <code>linger.ms</code> to something greater than 0. This will\\n142:  * instruct the producer to wait up to that number of milliseconds before sending a request in hope that more records will\\n143:  * arrive to fill up the same batch. This is analogous to Nagle's algorithm in TCP. For example, in the code snippet above,\\n144:  * likely all 100 records would be sent in a single request since we set our linger time to 1 millisecond. However this setting\\n145:  * would add 1 millisecond of latency to our request waiting for more records to arrive if we didn't fill up the buffer. Note that\\n146:  * records that arrive close together in time will generally batch together even with <code>linger.ms=0</code>. So, under heavy load,\\n147:  * batching will occur regardless of the linger configuration; however setting this to something larger than 0 can lead to fewer, more\\n148:  * efficient requests when not under maximal load at the cost of a small amount of latency.\\n149:  * <p>\\n150:  * The <code>buffer.memory</code> controls the total amount of memory available to the producer for buffering. If records\\n151:  * are sent faster than they can be transmitted to the server then this buffer space will be exhausted. When the buffer space is\\n152:  * exhausted additional send calls will block. The threshold for time to block is determined by <code>max.block.ms</code> after which it returns\\n153:  * a failed future with BufferExhaustedException.\\n154:  * <p>\\n155:  * The <code>key.serializer</code> and <code>value.serializer</code> instruct how to turn the key and value objects the user provides with\\n156:  * their <code>ProducerRecord</code> into bytes. You can use the included {@link org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.ByteArraySerializer} or\\n157:  * {@link org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer} for simple byte or string types.\\n158:  * <p>\\n159:  * From Kafka 0.11, the KafkaProducer supports two additional modes: the idempotent producer and the transactional producer.\\n160:  * The idempotent producer strengthens Kafka's delivery semantics from at least once to exactly once delivery. In particular\\n161:  * producer retries will no longer introduce duplicates. The transactional producer allows an application to send messages\\n162:  * to multiple partitions (and topics!) atomically.\\n163:  * </p>\\n164:  * <p>\\n165:  * From Kafka 3.0, the <code>enable.idempotence</code> configuration defaults to true. When enabling idempotence,\\n166:  * <code>retries</code> config will default to <code>Integer.MAX_VALUE</code> and the <code>acks</code> config will\\n167:  * default to <code>all</code>. There are no API changes for the idempotent producer, so existing applications will\\n168:  * not need to be modified to take advantage of this feature.\\n169:  * </p>\\n170:  * <p>\\n171:  * To take advantage of the idempotent producer, it is imperative to avoid application level re-sends since these cannot\\n172:  * be de-duplicated. As such, if an application enables idempotence, it is recommended to leave the <code>retries</code>\\n173:  * config unset, as it will be defaulted to <code>Integer.MAX_VALUE</code>. Additionally, if a {@link #send(ProducerRecord)}\\n174:  * returns an error even with infinite retries (for instance if the message expires in the buffer before being sent),\\n175:  * then it is recommended to shut down the producer and check the contents of the last produced message to ensure that\\n176:  * it is not duplicated. Finally, the producer can only guarantee idempotence for messages sent within a single session.\\n177:  * </p>\\n178:  * <p>To use the transactional producer and the attendant APIs, you must set the <code>transactional.id</code>\\n179:  * configuration property. If the <code>transactional.id</code> is set, idempotence is automatically enabled along with\\n180:  * the producer configs which idempotence depends on. Further, topics which are included in transactions should be configured\\n181:  * for durability. In particular, the <code>replication.factor</code> should be at least <code>3</code>, and the\\n182:  * <code>min.insync.replicas</code> for these topics should be set to 2. Finally, in order for transactional guarantees\\n183:  * to be realized from end-to-end, the consumers must be configured to read only committed messages as well.\\n184:  * </p>\\n185:  * <p>\\n186:  * The purpose of the <code>transactional.id</code> is to enable transaction recovery across multiple sessions of a\\n187:  * single producer instance. It would typically be derived from the shard identifier in a partitioned, stateful, application.\\n188:  * As such, it should be unique to each producer instance running within a partitioned application.\\n189:  * </p>\\n190:  * <p>All the new transactional APIs are blocking and will throw exceptions on failure. The example\\n191:  * below illustrates how the new APIs are meant to be used. It is similar to the example above, except that all\\n192:  * 100 messages are part of a single transaction.\\n193:  * </p>\\n194:  * <p>\\n195:  * <pre>\\n196:  * {@code\\n197:  * Properties props = new Properties();\\n198:  * props.put(\\\"bootstrap.servers\\\", \\\"localhost:9092\\\");\\n199:  * props.put(\\\"transactional.id\\\", \\\"my-transactional-id\\\");\\n200:  * Producer<String, String> producer = new KafkaProducer<>(props, new StringSerializer(), new StringSerializer());\\n201:  *\\n202:  * producer.initTransactions();\\n203:  *\\n204:  * try {\\n205:  *     producer.beginTransaction();\\n206:  *     for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)\\n207:  *         producer.send(new ProducerRecord<>(\\\"my-topic\\\", Integer.toString(i), Integer.toString(i)));\\n208:  *     producer.commitTransaction();\\n209:  * } catch (ProducerFencedException | OutOfOrderSequenceException | AuthorizationException e) {\\n210:  *     // We can't recover from these exceptions, so our only option is to close the producer and exit.\\n211:  *     producer.close();\\n212:  * } catch (KafkaException e) {\\n213:  *     // For all other exceptions, just abort the transaction and try again.\\n214:  *     producer.abortTransaction();\\n215:  * }\\n216:  * producer.close();\\n217:  * } </pre>\\n218:  * </p>\\n219:  * <p>\\n220:  * As is hinted at in the example, there can be only one open transaction per producer. All messages sent between the\\n221:  * {@link #beginTransaction()} and {@link #commitTransaction()} calls will be part of a single transaction. When the\\n222:  * <code>transactional.id</code> is specified, all messages sent by the producer must be part of a transaction.\\n223:  * </p>\\n224:  * <p>\\n225:  * The transactional producer uses exceptions to communicate error states. In particular, it is not required\\n226:  * to specify callbacks for <code>producer.send()</code> or to call <code>.get()</code> on the returned Future: a\\n227:  * <code>KafkaException</code> would be thrown if any of the\\n228:  * <code>producer.send()</code> or transactional calls hit an irrecoverable error during a transaction. See the {@link #send(ProducerRecord)}\\n229:  * documentation for more details about detecting errors from a transactional send.\\n230:  * </p>\\n231:  * </p>By calling\\n232:  * <code>producer.abortTransaction()</code> upon receiving a <code>KafkaException</code> we can ensure that any\\n233:  * successful writes are marked as aborted, hence keeping the transactional guarantees.\\n234:  * </p>\\n235:  * <p>\\n236:  * This client can communicate with brokers that are version 0.10.0 or newer. Older or newer brokers may not support\\n237:  * certain client features.  For instance, the transactional APIs need broker versions 0.11.0 or later. You will receive an\\n238:  * <code>UnsupportedVersionException</code> when invoking an API that is not available in the running broker version.\\n239:  * </p>\\n240:  */\\n241: public class KafkaProducer<K, V> implements Producer<K, V> {\\n242: \\n243:     private final Logger log;\\n244:     private static final String JMX_PREFIX = \\\"kafka.producer\\\";\\n245:     public static final String NETWORK_THREAD_PREFIX = \\\"kafka-producer-network-thread\\\";\\n246:     public static final String PRODUCER_METRIC_GROUP_NAME = \\\"producer-metrics\\\";\\n247: \\n248:     private final String clientId;\\n249:     // Visible for testing\\n250:     final Metrics metrics;\\n251: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/kafka--0753c489@31d9200189f81283bf60e929ddc38fd68ac034e2/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/KafkaProducer.java%3FL50-250\"}","structuredContent":{"content":"50: However this setting\n145:  * would add 1 millisecond of latency to our request waiting for more records to arrive if we didn't fill up the buffer. If records\n151:  * are sent faster than they can be transmitted to the server then this buffer space will be exhausted. When the buffer space is\n152:  * exhausted additional send calls will block. In particular\n161:  * producer retries will no longer introduce duplicates. The transactional producer allows an application to send messages\n162:  * to multiple partitions (and topics!) atomically.\n163:  * </p>\n164:  * <p>\n165:  * From Kafka 3.0, the <code>enable.idempotence</code> configuration defaults to true. Additionally, if a {@link #send(ProducerRecord)}\n174:  * returns an error even with infinite retries (for instance if the message expires in the buffer before being sent),\n175:  * then it is recommended to shut down the producer and check the contents of the last produced message to ensure that\n176:  * it is not duplicated. Finally, the producer can only guarantee idempotence for messages sent within a single session.\n177:  * </p>\n178:  * <p>To use the transactional producer and the attendant APIs, you must set the <code>transactional.id</code>\n179:  * configuration property. If the <code>transactional.id</code> is set, idempotence is automatically enabled along with\n180:  * the producer configs which idempotence depends on. Further, topics which are included in transactions should be configured\n181:  * for durability. In particular, the <code>replication.factor</code> should be at least <code>3</code>, and the\n182:  * <code>min.insync.replicas</code> for these topics should be set to 2. Finally, in order for transactional guarantees\n183:  * to be realized from end-to-end, the consumers must be configured to read only committed messages as well.\n184:  * </p>\n185:  * <p>\n186:  * The purpose of the <code>transactional.id</code> is to enable transaction recovery across multiple sessions of a\n187:  * single producer instance. It would typically be derived from the shard identifier in a partitioned, stateful, application.\n188:  * As such, it should be unique to each producer instance running within a partitioned application.\n189:  * </p>\n190:  * <p>All the new transactional APIs are blocking and will throw exceptions on failure. The example\n191:  * below illustrates how the new APIs are meant to be used. It is similar to the example above, except that all\n192:  * 100 messages are part of a single transaction.\n193:  * </p>\n194:  * <p>\n195:  * <pre>\n196:  * {@code\n197:  * Properties props = new Properties();\n198:  * props.put(\"bootstrap.servers\", \"localhost:9092\");\n199:  * props.put(\"transactional.id\", \"my-transactional-id\");\n200:  * Producer<String, String> producer = new KafkaProducer<>(props, new StringSerializer(), new StringSerializer());\n201:  *\n202:  * producer.initTransactions();\n203:  *\n204:  * try {\n205:  *     producer.beginTransaction();\n206:  *     for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)\n207:  *         producer.send(new ProducerRecord<>(\"my-topic\", Integer.toString(i), Integer.toString(i)));\n208:  *     producer.commitTransaction();\n209:  * } catch (ProducerFencedException | OutOfOrderSequenceException | AuthorizationException e) {\n210:  *     // We can't recover from these exceptions, so our only option is to close the producer and exit.\n211:  *     producer.close();\n212:  * } catch (KafkaException e) {\n213:  *     // For all other exceptions, just abort the transaction and try again.\n214:  *     producer.abortTransaction();\n215:  * }\n216:  * producer.close();\n217:  * } </pre>\n218:  * </p>\n219:  * <p>\n220:  * As is hinted at in the example, there can be only one open transaction per producer. All messages sent between the\n221:  * {@link #beginTransaction()} and {@link #commitTransaction()} calls will be part of a single transaction. When the\n222:  * <code>transactional.id</code> is specified, all messages sent by the producer must be part of a transaction.\n223:  * </p>\n224:  * <p>\n225:  * The transactional producer uses exceptions to communicate error states. In particular, it is not required\n226:  * to specify callbacks for <code>producer.send()</code> or to call <code>.get()</code> on the returned Future: a\n227:  * <code>KafkaException</code> would be thrown if any of the\n228:  * <code>producer.send()</code> or transactional calls hit an irrecoverable error during a transaction. refactoring: the class `RecordAccumulator` is renamed to `BatchAccumulator` while all observable behaviour, method signatures, and inner-class interfaces remain identical. The rename is justified because the class accumulates `ProducerBatch` objects (not individual records) in per-partition deques, and its primary methods (`append`, `drain`, `ready`) all operate at batch granularity.\n\n### Affected Areas\n\n**Production code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulator.java` → `BatchAccumulator.java`: The source of truth. Two constructor signatures and the logger `logger(Class)` call must reference the new name.\n- `KafkaProducer.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor that creates a `BatchAccumulator`, one overloaded test-visible constructor that accepts a `BatchAccumulator`, and two inner-type references (`PartitionerConfig`, `RecordAppendResult`).\n- `Sender.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor parameter, one inner-type reference (`ReadyCheckResult`).\n- `BuiltInPartitioner.java` & `Node.java`: Comment-only changes; no compilation impact.\n\n**Test code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorTest.java` → `BatchAccumulatorTest.java`: File and class renamed; factory methods renamed; `new RecordAccumulator(...)` constructor calls updated.\n- `SenderTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls, and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.PartitionerConfig` usages.\n- `TransactionManagerTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls.\n- `KafkaProducerTest.java`: Import and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` inner-type references.\n\n**Benchmark (1 file):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java` → `BatchAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java`: File and class renamed; field type and factory method renamed.\n\n**Checkstyle (1 file):**\n- `suppressions.xml`: Three regex patterns that include `RecordAccumulator` must be updated to `BatchAccumulator` to keep suppressions effective.\n\n### Inner Class Names\n\nThe inner classes `RecordAppendResult`, `ReadyCheckResult`, `PartitionerConfig`, `AppendCallbacks`, and `NodeLatencyStats` are **not renamed** — the task specification only asks to rename the outer class. Their qualified names change from `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc. to `BatchAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc., but the simple names are preserved.\n\n### Verification Approach\n\nAfter applying all changes:\n1. `grep -r \"RecordAccumulator\" clients/ jmh-benchmarks/ checkstyle/` should return only `ProducerBatch.java` (which has a Javadoc comment referencing `RecordAccumulator` in the context of the old name — this is a comment-only reference that can optionally also be updated) and no compilation-relevant references.\n2. refactoring: the class `RecordAccumulator` is renamed to `BatchAccumulator` while all observable behaviour, method signatures, and inner-class interfaces remain identical. The rename is justified because the class accumulates `ProducerBatch` objects (not individual records) in per-partition deques, and its primary methods (`append`, `drain`, `ready`) all operate at batch granularity.\n\n### Affected Areas\n\n**Production code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulator.java` → `BatchAccumulator.java`: The source of truth. Two constructor signatures and the logger `logger(Class)` call must reference the new name.\n- `KafkaProducer.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor that creates a `BatchAccumulator`, one overloaded test-visible constructor that accepts a `BatchAccumulator`, and two inner-type references (`PartitionerConfig`, `RecordAppendResult`).\n- `Sender.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor parameter, one inner-type reference (`ReadyCheckResult`).\n- `BuiltInPartitioner.java` & `Node.java`: Comment-only changes; no compilation impact.\n\n**Test code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorTest.java` → `BatchAccumulatorTest.java`: File and class renamed; factory methods renamed; `new RecordAccumulator(...)` constructor calls updated.\n- `SenderTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls, and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.PartitionerConfig` usages.\n- `TransactionManagerTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls.\n- `KafkaProducerTest.java`: Import and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` inner-type references.\n\n**Benchmark (1 file):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java` → `BatchAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java`: File and class renamed; field type and factory method renamed.\n\n**Checkstyle (1 file):**\n- `suppressions.xml`: Three regex patterns that include `RecordAccumulator` must be updated to `BatchAccumulator` to keep suppressions effective.\n\n### Inner Class Names\n\nThe inner classes `RecordAppendResult`, `ReadyCheckResult`, `PartitionerConfig`, `AppendCallbacks`, and `NodeLatencyStats` are **not renamed** — the task specification only asks to rename the outer class. Their qualified names change from `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc. to `BatchAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc., but the simple names are preserved.\n\n### Verification Approach\n\nAfter applying all changes:\n1. `grep -r \"RecordAccumulator\" clients/ jmh-benchmarks/ checkstyle/` should return only `ProducerBatch.java` (which has a Javadoc comment referencing `RecordAccumulator` in the context of the old name — this is a comment-only reference that can optionally also be updated) and no compilation-relevant references.\n2. The full producer test suite (`./gradlew :clients:test --tests \"*BatchAccumulatorTest\"`, `\"*SenderTest\"`, `\"*TransactionManagerTest\"`, `\"*KafkaProducerTest\"`) should compile and pass without modification to the test logic, since only names changed.\n3. The JMH benchmark module should compile with the renamed class.\n","structuredPatch":[],"originalFile":null}}

refactoring: the class `RecordAccumulator` is renamed to `BatchAccumulator` while all observable behaviour, method signatures, and inner-class interfaces remain identical. The rename is justified because the class accumulates `ProducerBatch` objects (not individual records) in per-partition deques, and its primary methods (`append`, `drain`, `ready`) all operate at batch granularity.\n\n### Affected Areas\n\n**Production code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulator.java` → `BatchAccumulator.java`: The source of truth. Two constructor signatures and the logger `logger(Class)` call must reference the new name.\n- `KafkaProducer.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor that creates a `BatchAccumulator`, one overloaded test-visible constructor that accepts a `BatchAccumulator`, and two inner-type references (`PartitionerConfig`, `RecordAppendResult`).\n- `Sender.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor parameter, one inner-type reference (`ReadyCheckResult`).\n- `BuiltInPartitioner.java` & `Node.java`: Comment-only changes; no compilation impact.\n\n**Test code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorTest.java` → `BatchAccumulatorTest.java`: File and class renamed; factory methods renamed; `new RecordAccumulator(...)` constructor calls updated.\n- `SenderTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls, and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.PartitionerConfig` usages.\n- `TransactionManagerTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls.\n- `KafkaProducerTest.java`: Import and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` inner-type references.\n\n**Benchmark (1 file):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java` → `BatchAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java`: File and class renamed; field type and factory method renamed.\n\n**Checkstyle (1 file):**\n- `suppressions.xml`: Three regex patterns that include `RecordAccumulator` must be updated to `BatchAccumulator` to keep suppressions effective.\n\n### Inner Class Names\n\nThe inner classes `RecordAppendResult`, `ReadyCheckResult`, `PartitionerConfig`, `AppendCallbacks`, and `NodeLatencyStats` are **not renamed** — the task specification only asks to rename the outer class. Their qualified names change from `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc. to `BatchAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc., but the simple names are preserved.\n\n### Verification Approach\n\nAfter applying all changes:\n1. `grep -r \"RecordAccumulator\" clients/ jmh-benchmarks/ checkstyle/` should return only `ProducerBatch.java` (which has a Javadoc comment referencing `RecordAccumulator` in the context of the old name — this is a comment-only reference that can optionally also be updated) and no compilation-relevant references.\n2. The full producer test suite (`./gradlew :clients:test --tests \"*BatchAccumulatorTest\"`, `\"*SenderTest\"`, `\"*TransactionManagerTest\"`, `\"*KafkaProducerTest\"`) should compile and pass without modification to the test logic, since only names changed.\n3. The JMH benchmark module should compile with the renamed class.\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":10,"oldLines":6,"newStart":10,"newLines":7,"lines":[" - `clients/src/test/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/TransactionManagerTest.java` — imports `RecordAccumulator` and instantiates it directly"," - `clients/src/test/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/KafkaProducerTest.java` — imports `RecordAccumulator` and references inner types `AppendCallbacks` and `RecordAppendResult`"," - `jmh-benchmarks/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/jmh/producer/RecordAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java` — benchmark class that imports and directly uses `RecordAccumulator`; needs renaming to `BatchAccumulatorFlushBenchmark`","+- `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/ProducerBatch.java` — Javadoc comment on line 530 uses `{@link RecordAccumulator}` to reference the class by name"," - `checkstyle/suppressions.xml` — suppression entries reference the filename `RecordAccumulator.java` in three regex patterns for `ParameterNumber`, `CyclomaticComplexity`, and `NPathComplexity` checks"," "," ## Dependency Chain"]}],"userModified":false,"replaceAll":false}}

refactoring: the class `RecordAccumulator` is renamed to `BatchAccumulator` while all observable behaviour, method signatures, and inner-class interfaces remain identical. The rename is justified because the class accumulates `ProducerBatch` objects (not individual records) in per-partition deques, and its primary methods (`append`, `drain`, `ready`) all operate at batch granularity.\n\n### Affected Areas\n\n**Production code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulator.java` → `BatchAccumulator.java`: The source of truth. Two constructor signatures and the logger `logger(Class)` call must reference the new name.\n- `KafkaProducer.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor that creates a `BatchAccumulator`, one overloaded test-visible constructor that accepts a `BatchAccumulator`, and two inner-type references (`PartitionerConfig`, `RecordAppendResult`).\n- `Sender.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor parameter, one inner-type reference (`ReadyCheckResult`).\n- `BuiltInPartitioner.java` & `Node.java`: Comment-only changes; no compilation impact.\n\n**Test code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorTest.java` → `BatchAccumulatorTest.java`: File and class renamed; factory methods renamed; `new RecordAccumulator(...)` constructor calls updated.\n- `SenderTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls, and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.PartitionerConfig` usages.\n- `TransactionManagerTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls.\n- `KafkaProducerTest.java`: Import and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` inner-type references.\n\n**Benchmark (1 file):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java` → `BatchAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java`: File and class renamed; field type and factory method renamed.\n\n**Checkstyle (1 file):**\n- `suppressions.xml`: Three regex patterns that include `RecordAccumulator` must be updated to `BatchAccumulator` to keep suppressions effective.\n\n### Inner Class Names\n\nThe inner classes `RecordAppendResult`, `ReadyCheckResult`, `PartitionerConfig`, `AppendCallbacks`, and `NodeLatencyStats` are **not renamed** — the task specification only asks to rename the outer class. Their qualified names change from `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc. to `BatchAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc., but the simple names are preserved.\n\n### Verification Approach\n\nAfter applying all changes:\n1. `grep -r \"RecordAccumulator\" clients/ jmh-benchmarks/ checkstyle/` should return only `ProducerBatch.java` (which has a Javadoc comment referencing `RecordAccumulator` in the context of the old name — this is a comment-only reference that can optionally also be updated) and no compilation-relevant references.\n2. The full producer test suite (`./gradlew :clients:test --tests \"*BatchAccumulatorTest\"`, `\"*SenderTest\"`, `\"*TransactionManagerTest\"`, `\"*KafkaProducerTest\"`) should compile and pass without modification to the test logic, since only names changed.\n3. The JMH benchmark module should compile with the renamed class.\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":20,"oldLines":7,"newStart":20,"newLines":8,"lines":[" 3. **Direct usage (production)**: `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/Sender.java` — imports `RecordAccumulator`, holds it as a constructor parameter and field, uses `ReadyCheckResult`"," 4. **Comment reference**: `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/BuiltInPartitioner.java` — Javadoc comments name `RecordAccumulator` as the context class"," 5. **Comment reference**: `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/Node.java` — inline comment references `RecordAccumulator.ready`","-6. **Test**: `clients/src/test/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/RecordAccumulatorTest.java` — tests the class directly","+6. **Comment reference**: `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/ProducerBatch.java` — Javadoc uses `{@link RecordAccumulator}` on line 530","+7. **Test**: `clients/src/test/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/RecordAccumulatorTest.java` — tests the class directly"," 7. **Transitive (via Sender)**: `clients/src/test/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/SenderTest.java` — creates `RecordAccumulator` instances to test Sender"," 8. **Transitive (via TransactionManager + Sender)**: `clients/src/test/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/TransactionManagerTest.java` — creates `RecordAccumulator` instances to test transaction manager via Sender"," 9. **Transitive (via KafkaProducer)**: `clients/src/test/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/KafkaProducerTest.java` — mocks and references `RecordAccumulator` inner types"]}],"userModified":false,"replaceAll":false}}

refactoring: the class `RecordAccumulator` is renamed to `BatchAccumulator` while all observable behaviour, method signatures, and inner-class interfaces remain identical. The rename is justified because the class accumulates `ProducerBatch` objects (not individual records) in per-partition deques, and its primary methods (`append`, `drain`, `ready`) all operate at batch granularity.\n\n### Affected Areas\n\n**Production code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulator.java` → `BatchAccumulator.java`: The source of truth. Two constructor signatures and the logger `logger(Class)` call must reference the new name.\n- `KafkaProducer.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor that creates a `BatchAccumulator`, one overloaded test-visible constructor that accepts a `BatchAccumulator`, and two inner-type references (`PartitionerConfig`, `RecordAppendResult`).\n- `Sender.java`: One import, one field declaration, one constructor parameter, one inner-type reference (`ReadyCheckResult`).\n- `BuiltInPartitioner.java` & `Node.java`: Comment-only changes; no compilation impact.\n\n**Test code (4 files):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorTest.java` → `BatchAccumulatorTest.java`: File and class renamed; factory methods renamed; `new RecordAccumulator(...)` constructor calls updated.\n- `SenderTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls, and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.PartitionerConfig` usages.\n- `TransactionManagerTest.java`: Import, field type, all `new RecordAccumulator(...)` calls.\n- `KafkaProducerTest.java`: Import and `RecordAccumulator.AppendCallbacks` / `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` inner-type references.\n\n**Benchmark (1 file):**\n- `RecordAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java` → `BatchAccumulatorFlushBenchmark.java`: File and class renamed; field type and factory method renamed.\n\n**Checkstyle (1 file):**\n- `suppressions.xml`: Three regex patterns that include `RecordAccumulator` must be updated to `BatchAccumulator` to keep suppressions effective.\n\n### Inner Class Names\n\nThe inner classes `RecordAppendResult`, `ReadyCheckResult`, `PartitionerConfig`, `AppendCallbacks`, and `NodeLatencyStats` are **not renamed** — the task specification only asks to rename the outer class. Their qualified names change from `RecordAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc. to `BatchAccumulator.RecordAppendResult` etc., but the simple names are preserved.\n\n### Verification Approach\n\nAfter applying all changes:\n1. `grep -r \"RecordAccumulator\" clients/ jmh-benchmarks/ checkstyle/` should return only `ProducerBatch.java` (which has a Javadoc comment referencing `RecordAccumulator` in the context of the old name — this is a comment-only reference that can optionally also be updated) and no compilation-relevant references.\n2. The full producer test suite (`./gradlew :clients:test --tests \"*BatchAccumulatorTest\"`, `\"*SenderTest\"`, `\"*TransactionManagerTest\"`, `\"*KafkaProducerTest\"`) should compile and pass without modification to the test logic, since only names changed.\n3. The JMH benchmark module should compile with the renamed class.\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":144,"oldLines":6,"newStart":144,"newLines":13,"lines":[" +    // Cache hashCode as it is called in performance sensitive parts of the code (e.g. BatchAccumulator.ready)"," ```"," ","+### clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/producer/internals/ProducerBatch.java","+","+```diff","+-     * when aborting batches in {@link RecordAccumulator}).","++     * when aborting batches in {@link BatchAccumulator}).","+```","+"," ### checkstyle/suppressions.xml"," "," ```diff"]}],"userModified":false,"replaceAll":false}}
